flashcards.front: What distinguishes continuous random variables from discrete ones?
flashcards.back: Continuous variables can take any value within an interval (uncountable), while discrete variables have countable distinct values.

flashcards.front: What is the total area under a probability density function (PDF) curve?
flashcards.back: Always equals 1, representing total probability over the sample space.

flashcards.front: Name three common continuous distributions in computing.
How is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) related to PDF?
flashcards.back: CDF is the integral of PDF from -∞ to x: F(x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt

flashcards.front: What does the Expected Value (E[X]) represent for continuous variables?
flashcards.back: The long-run average value of repetitions of the experiment it models.

flashcards.front: Why can't we assign probabilities to individual values in continuous distributions?
flashcards.back: Because there are infinitely many possible values (P(X=x)=0 for any x).

flashcards.front: What distribution models time between independent events?
How does variance relate to distribution spread?
flashcards.back: Higher variance means wider spread of values around the mean.

flashcards.front: What transformation converts Normal to Standard Normal distribution?
What is the PDF of Uniform distribution over [a,b]?
Why are continuous variables crucial in sensor data analysis?
flashcards.back: Sensor readings (temperature, pressure) are inherently continuous measurements.
Percentage of data within 1/2/3σ of mean in Normal distribution.

flashcards.front: How is network latency typically modeled?
What key property makes Normal distribution ubiquitous?
flashcards.back: Central Limit Theorem: sums of variables tend to be Normal.
